titleTag: Florida Landlord Tenant Law 2026 | Rights, Deposits & Eviction
metaDescription: Florida landlord tenant law 2026: security deposit limits, eviction notice requirements, tenant rights & more. Plain-language guide for new landlords.

sections.heading: Security deposit rules
body: If you're looking for Florida security deposit rules, focus on (1) whether there is a cap, (2) when the deposit must be returned, and (3) whether an itemized statement is required.
bullets: Deposit cap: exceed 4
Return deadline: 30 days after move-out/tenancy end (deductions can affect details).
Itemized statement: required if deductions are taken (check deadlines and delivery method).
Deductions: Varies — confirm current statute.
Penalty risk: Varies — confirm current statute.
Interest: Varies — confirm current statute.

sections.heading: Eviction notice requirements
body: For Florida eviction notice requirements, the notice period often depends on why the eviction is being pursued (nonpayment vs lease violation vs holdover). Always use the correct notice form and follow the court process.
bullets: Nonpayment notice: commonly 15 days (verify by tenancy type/notice form).
Nonpayment notice type: Varies — confirm required notice form (pay-or-quit, demand for possession, etc.).
Lease violation notice: commonly 15 days (may allow cure).
Lease violation notice type: Varies — confirm notice type and cure rules.
Cure allowed: varies
Month-to-month termination: commonly 7 days (verify tenancy type).
Holdover/termination notice type: Varies — confirm termination/holdover notice rules.
Court process: varies — confirm court process requirements and exceptions.
Self-help eviction: varies — confirm current statute and case law.
Timeline:
Court steps: Fla. Stat. § 83.56. TERMINATION OF RENTAL AGREEMENT.

sections.heading: Landlord entry notice
body: If you're searching for Florida landlord entry notice rules, look for (1) required notice time, (2) allowed entry reasons, and (3) emergency exceptions.
bullets: Notice: often governed by lease terms and a reasonableness standard (state law may not set a single hour-count).
Typical notice time: varies; many leases use 24 hours as a practical baseline.

Allowed reasons: (1) The tenant shall not unreasonably withhold consent to the landlord to enter the dwelling unit from time to time in order to inspect the premises; make necessary or agreed repairs, decorations, alterations, or improvements; supply agreed services; or exhibit the dwelling unit to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workers, or contractors. (1) The tenant shall not unreasonably withhold consent to the landlord to enter the dwelling unit from time to time in order to inspect the premises; make necessary or agreed repairs, decorations, alterations, or improvements; supply agreed services; or exhibit the dwelling unit to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workers, or contractors.
Timing: Varies — usually at reasonable times.
Emergencies: generally allow entry without advance notice.
Tenant refusal: tenant cannot unreasonably withhold consent
Remedies/penalties: Varies — confirm remedies (injunction, damages, attorney fees) under state law.

sections.heading: Rent increase rules
body: For Florida rent increase rules, most states focus on notice requirements, while rent caps are usually local (or limited to specific covered housing).
bullets: Statewide rent control: typically none, but local/covered-housing rules may apply.
Local rent control: may exist in certain cities/counties (check if your property is covered).
Notice of rent increase: varies — confirm current statute and any local ordinances.
Fixed-term leases: Varies — confirm your lease terms and statute.
Month-to-month: Varies — generally allowed with proper notice.
Delivery: Varies — confirm written notice and delivery method requirements.
Just cause: varies
Retaliation: Retaliation protections may limit rent increases after protected tenant complaints; confirm local/state rules.

sections.heading: Tenant rights & habitability basics
body: Most Florida landlord tenant law disputes involve habitability, repairs, and retaliation protections. Keep repair requests documented and respond promptly.
bullets: Habitability: varies — confirm current law
Landlord repair duty: (a) Comply with the requirements of applicable building, housing, and health codes; or

Tenant notice: 1. The extermination of rats, mice, roaches, ants, wood-destroying organisms, and bedbugs. When vacation of the premises is required for such extermination, the landlord shall not be liable for damages but shall abate the rent. The landlord must provide reasonable notice of not less than 7 days with a written notice of the need to vacate for a period not to exceed 4 days;
Repair-and-deduct: varies
Rent withholding: varies
Termination: Varies — some states allow termination after notice and failure to repair.
Retaliation: varies
Code enforcement: Varies — contact local housing/code enforcement (city/county) for inspections and official repair orders.

jurisdictionNotes: Local ordinances (city/county) can be stricter than state law.
Rules can change; verify before acting on any single requirement.
confidenceNotes: This page is being refreshed for accuracy; key limits and notice periods are verified state-by-state during the rollout.
